Platoon

Document I · Version 2.0 · D. Rodriguez
New Guy Training (NGT)
Onboarding training covering kit setup, keybindings, medical SOP, and basic small unit tactics for Ghost Platoon recruits.
Section I — Basic Kit & Weapon Platforms
Ace Arsenal

Trainees establish their basic rifleman kit in Ace Arsenal. Uniform standard is MC with GPLT Patch. Rifle platform runs 5.56 NATO.

⊕ Uniform (Medical)
19× Bandage (Elastic)
8× Bandage (QuikClot)
1× Chest Seal
5× Epi
2× King LT
4× Tourniquet
2× Painkiller
Blood (500ml)
Pulse Oximeter
2× Splints
⊕ Vest
1× AN/PRC-152 Radio
9+1× Primary Rifle Mags
2+1× Secondary Mags
Blood (500ml)
2× Cable Ties
1× Galaxy S7 ATAK
Vector 21 Nite
Compass
GPS
Map
Altimeter Watch
⊕ Backpack
1× MicroDAGR GPS
1× NVGs (of choice)
1× Lock Popper
1× Stick Charge
1× M152 Firing Device
1× Mine Detector
1× Entrenching Tool
1× Green Spray Paint
2× IR Strobe
1× IFAK
5× Cable Ties

MAAWS Mk4 Mod 1

Section II
Keybindings
Changes ease mind-to-key mapping. Some defaults conflict; others are safety-critical.
Default Keybinds
CategoryAction & Binding
CommonUnbind "last help" key
WeaponsRebind "throw" → G G
Infantry MovementsRebind "step over" → CTRL+V
Enhanced Movement ReworkAll-in-one action → SHIFT+V
AddOn Keybinds
ModBinding
Ace CommonOpen/Close Door → Unbind
ACE MovementUnbind All
CIA SAC/SOG ModsKick door → CTRL+SPACE
cTabMain Interface → CTRL+H
Secondary → SHIFT+H
Rads Walking SpeedSet Speed Max → C
TR21 Chemlight DropGreen → CTRL+4 · Blue → CTRL+5 · IR → CTRL+6
Section III
ACE Medical Training
Self Aid
Press H
  • Finish Fight
  • Stop Bleeding
  • Seek Medic Aid
Buddy Aid
Press H
  • Finish Fight
  • Check the Scene
  • Move Patient to Secure Area
  • Stop Bleeding
  • Airway Management
  • Stitch if Able
  • Check Fluids (no TQ limb IV)
  • Check Heart Rate
  • If None: Epi then CPR
  • Wait and Supervise
Section IV
Formations
Wedge
ProImmediate fires in all directions
ConRough terrain / poor visibility
UseOpen terrain
Column
ProImmediate fire left and right
ConLimited security front/rear
UseWooded terrain (10m spacing)
Staggered Column
ProSecurity
ConLong to move on line
UseRoads / urban terrain
Line
ProMaximum firepower to front
ConMinimum fire left/right
UseReacting to contact
Echelon L/R
ProHigh firepower one side
ConDisadvantaged to opposite side
UseExpecting fire from specific squad
Diamond
Pro360° security / HVT protection
ConHard to maintain spacing
UseProtect HVTs
Document II · Version 2.0 · Ghost Platoon Document Team
Battle Drills
14 battle drills covering direct fire contact, assaults, obstacle breaching, IED response, and vehicle operations. Click any drill to expand.
Document III · Version 2.0 · Ghost Platoon Document Team
AARs — A Small Unit Perspective
The After-Action Review is a professional discussion enabling units and leaders to understand why events occurred and adapt accordingly.
What is an AAR?

An after-action review is an assessment conducted after an event or major activity that allows participants to learn what and why something happened — and how the unit can improve through change.

Advantages over a critique
  • Does not judge success or failure
  • Attempts to discover why things happened
  • Focuses directly on the tasks and goals to be accomplished
  • Encourages participants to recall more and share lessons learned

The AAR may be conducted after each identifiable event as a live learning process — not only at mission end.

Phase 01
The Plan — Review What Was Supposed to Happen

A concise restatement of the operation order (OPORD). The patrol leader stands in front of the team and restates the commander's intent and scheme of maneuver.

No conjecture or grandstanding — simply a restatement of the plan. Begin by asking each element leader what mission they were tasked with, in their own words. Listen for clarity of focus and understanding of their role.

Phase 02
The Performance — Establish What Happened

Those on the ground state what really happened. The PL is generally discouraged from speaking unless they hold unique perspective. The goal is to find the gap between the plan and actual performance.

Start with initial movement: Did the patrol hit their start time? Time hacks for phase lines or ORP? Address actions in the ORP and on the objective. Most issues fall within each element's ability to move, shoot, and communicate.

Phase 03
The Issues — Determining What Was Right or Wrong

Identify reasons for the discrepancy between plan and performance. Allow venting — both positive and negative. Invite everyone to speak their mind.

Use the "nut shelling" technique: alternate between asking the most vocal member what needs to be a future training focus, and what was well executed. Listen for consensus. Make the most negative troops give a positive observation, and vice versa.

Phase 04
The Fix — Determine What Should Be Done Differently

Identify by name who is responsible for correcting each deficiency. Do not assume all responsibility falls on the PL. Subordinate leaders and experienced troops can lead training or take ownership of equipment.

Assign a leadership role in training, fix or acquire necessary equipment, or research the problem and report findings back to the team. Each issue must have an owner.

Ghost Platoon · 2/75th Ranger Regiment · Rangers Lead The Way, All The Way
